content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcar PnecrrcEs CouulssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
November 17,2003
Dennis G. Boom
9056 New Dawn Drive
Sacramento, CA 95826
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No.I-03-157
Dear Mr. Boom:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the post-
governmental employmentprovisions of the Political ReformAct (the..Act,).l Since
your request asks for general guidance and has not identified either a specific state
agency for which you will provide consulting seryices orthe nature of these consulting
services, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 please note that
nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already
taken place.
auEsTroNS
1. Do the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act bar you from
making software sales, or of[ering technical or management services, to stati agencies,
including your former state administrative agency employer (the Franchise TarBoard)?
After the one-year anniversary of your separation from state service, may you offer these
sales and services to the Franchise Tax Board?
2. Do the post-governmental ernployment provisions of the Act bar you from
accepting employment as a consultant or sales representative with a private iompany that
may do business with the State of California?3
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Corrmission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistrnce does not prolde the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal o1r$" advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3),copy enclosed.)
' These questions are taken from your request for u.ritten advice, as modified in your telephone
conversation with Commission staff, held on October 9,2003.
=#


File No. l-03-157
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. The one-yearban does not prohibit you from currently appeanngbefore or
communicating with state administrative agencies, other than your former state
administrative agency employer, the Franchise Tax Board. Thus, you are not prohibited
by the one-year ban from making software sales, or offering technical or management
services, to state administrative agencies, other than the Franchise Tax Board. This latter
prohibition expires on the close of the one-yeaf, period following your retirement from
state emplo5rment (i.e., May 1,20a4) and you may, thereafter, do business with the
Franchise Tax Board.
The permanent ban will prohibit you, however, when doing business as described
above, from advising or representing any person, other than the State of California, in any
proceeding in which you formerly participated while in state service. Because of your
former supervisory capacity, special rules apply to identiff the proceedings in which you
are deerned to have formerlyparticipated.
2. The post-governmental employment provisions of the Act do not bar you from
accepting employment as a consultant or sales representative with a private company that
may do business with the State of California. However, and as explained in greater detail
below, once you accept such emplo5rmant, the permanent ban potentially restricts the
assignments you may accqpt from your new employer and the one-year ban generally
prohibits your corrmunications or appearances before the Franchise Tax Board on behalf
of your new employer until after the one-year aruriversary of your separation from state
service.
FACTS
You are a former employee of the Franchise Tax Board ("FTB') where you were
employed for34 years. YourpositionuponyourMay l,z}O3,retirementwasdirectorof
that agency's Computer Resources Bureau. According to information you supplied, this
is a position that is designated in the FTB's conflict of interest code.a You are presently
contemplating offering private consulting services as an independent contractor to
various state agencies. You are also considering poturfial employment with a private
company, where you might be asked to sell the company's products to various state
agencies, including the FTB, or to provide consulting serices on that company's behalf.
ANALYSIS
State administrative officials5 who leave state service are subject to two types of
post-governmental employment restrictions under the Act:
{ This inforrnation was provided in your telephone conversation with the Commission's staff, held
onNovember 13,2003
5 A "state administrative official" is defined in section 87400(b) as "every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part ofhis or her official responsibilities
engages in anyjudicial, quasi-judicial or otherproceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or
ministerial capacity."


File No. I-03-157
Page No. 3
o A "one-yearbarr" prohibiting a state employee from
communicating with his or her former agency to influence the agency,s
adminishative or legislative action6 (section 87406); and
r A "permanent ban" barring a state employee from "switching sides,,
in any specific proceeding on which the employee worked while in state
service (sections 87 400-87 405).
A. The One-Year Ban
Section 87406(d)(1) states in pertinent part:
"No designated ernployee of a state administrative agency, any officer,
employee, or consultant of a state adminiskative agency who holds a
position which entails the making, orparticipation in the making, of
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest, and no member of a state administrative agency, for a period of
one year after leaving office or employment, shall, for compensation, act as
agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person, by making
any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication, before any state administratiye agerlcy, or officer or
employee thereof for which he or she worked orrepresented during the 12
months before leaving office or employment, if the appearance or
communication is made for the purpose of influencing administrative or
legislative action, or influencing any action orproceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant,
or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.,,
As the former director of the FTB's Computer Resources Bureau, yourposition
was designated in the FTB's conflict of interest code. Thus, you are subject to the one-
year ban.
"An appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by
telephone or in person, corresponding with in writing or by electronic transmission,
attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication.', (Regulation
18746.2, copy enclosed.) However, not all communications to a former state
6 "Influencing legislative or administrative action" includes influencing'by any m€ans, including
but not limited to the provision or use of information, statistics, studies or analysss.,'-(Section g2032.)
"Administrative action" is defined in section 82002 as "ttre proposal, drafting, ievelopmenq consideratiorl
amendment, enactnent, or defeat by any state agency of any ruie, regulation, or otheiaction in any
ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding . . . . " Section 82037 defines ..legislative
action" as "the drafting, inkoduction, consideration, modificatioq enactunent or defeat ofanf bill,
resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or any
committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature
acting in his official capacity. 'Legislative action' also means the action of the Governor in approvirg or
vetoing any bill."


File No. I-03-157
, 
Page No. 4
administrative agency employer are prohibited by the one-year ban; it is only when the
communication is for the purpose of "influencing" that the communication implicates the
one-year ban. An appearance or communication "is for the purpose of influencing if it is
made for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifyrng,
opposing, delaying, or advancing the action orproceeding." (Regulation rc7a6.2(a).)
You should be aware, however, that the one-year ban does not apply to services
performed "to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit,
license, grant, contract, or sale agreement . . . . provided the seryices do not involve the
issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of any of these actions orproceedings.,,
(Regulation 18746.1@XSXA); Hanan Advice Letter, No. I-00-209; Bilteci Advice Letter,
No. I-00-234.) Thus, the one-year ban does not apply if you were to be retained by
another person to provide consulting services that involve you exclusively in
administering, implementing, or fulfilling the requirements of an existing contract to
which the FTB is a party.
B. The Permanent Ban
Sections 87401 and' 87402 (collectively the "permanent ban') prohibit a former
state administrative official from advising or representing any person, other than the State
of California, for compensation in any judicial, quasi-judicial or otherproceeding in
which the official participated while in state service. A "judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding" includes a contract or other particular matter involving a specific party or
parties irl *y court or state administrative agency. (Section 87a00(c).) An ofEcial is
considered to have "particrpated" in a contract proceeding if the official was personally
and substantially involved in the contract. (Section 8740O(d).) Should you accept
employment with another employer, other than the State of California, the permanent ban
wlll apply to restrict you from advising orrepresenting that employer with regard to any
judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in which you participated while in state
service.T
To apply the permanent ban to your situation, you will need to identiff the
proceedings in which you particrpated while employed by the FTB. The permanent ban
does not apply to new or separate proceedings in which a former state agency employee
did not participate while in state service. (Section 57401.) There are special
considerations applicable to you in this regard since you held a management position at
the FTB. As a former manager, you are deemed to haveparticipated in an FTB
proceeding if: (1) the proceeding was pending before the FTB during your tenure , and 2)
7 Should the management services you would provide to a state administative agency qualiry you
as a consultant for that agency, as defined under regulation I 8701(a[2), you would be considered as
advising or representing an agency of the State of California. In such case, you are not prohibited by the
perrranent ban from providing these consulting services in a proceeding in which you formerlyparticipated
while in state service. Please note, however, that in tlese circumstances where you would not be an
employee or officer of a state adminishative agency, the one-year ban would still restrict your
communications and appearances before the Franchise Tax Board until after the anniversary date of your
separation from state service. (Section 87a06(e)(l).)


File No. I-03-157
Page No. 5
any decision regarding the proceeding was made by you directly or by someone under
your supervisory authority. (Regulation rcl a I J @)@).)8
"Supervisory authority''is not aphrase defined under the Act. This phrase was
adopted by the commission in the BrownAdvice Letter,No. A-91-033.e There, an
official was advised that he was deemed to have personally and substantially participated
in all proceedings ofhis former agency, if those proceedings were in his chain of
command during his tenure at the agency. In In re Lucas Q000) 14 FPPC Ops. 15, the
Commission clarified that not all proceedings subordinate to an official within his or her
chain of command are considered 'lrnder his or her supervisory authority." (Regulation
l874l.l(a)(a).) The commission concluded there that an official's general
administrative oversight of a program to be calried out by those subordinate to the
official on an agency's organizational chart was insufficient to rise to the level of
"personal and substantial" involvement required by the Act,
Thus, generally a proceeding is under an official's supervisory authority when the
proceeding is not only subordinate to the official under the agency's organizatironal
structure, but when the official also undertakes one ormore of the activities described in
section 87400(d) (copy enclosed). (Ericson, supra.)
Since you have not identified any specific FTB proceedings in which you were
formerly involved and do not identiff any specific assignments by a new employer, we
are unable to advise you other than in the general terrns above. Should you have
additional questions once specific facts are known, you may wish to write for further
advice.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (e16)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
By:
Counsel, Le gal Division
Enclosures
I<LGje
I:\Adviceltrs\03-157
8 This regulation is not meant to address situations where an official's acts are merely ministerial.
(Ericson Advice Lntter, No. I42-198.)
e lnBrown,the Commission considered a request from a former chief of its Enforcement Divisioq
concenring applicability of the permanent ban to his post-retirement representation in an enforcement
proceeding that commenced in the waning weeks ofhis state seryice. The Commission rejected his
argument that he was not personally and substantially involved in that proceeding since no substantive
work was undertaken either by himself or enforcement staffin the matter during his tenure.
J{^{

service.
